summary: Gruber reviews Apple's AirPods Max after a day and a half of use, finding them impressively built and great-sounding but notably heavy at 385 grams and expensive at $550. He praises their premium metal construction, spatial audio immersion, and simple no-EQ-fiddling philosophy, but notes the weight makes them unsuitable for anything beyond stationary use. His biggest frustration is that spatial audio only works with iPhone and iPad, not Apple TV — the device where premium headphone movie-watching makes the most sense. He concludes they're the best wireless headphones you can buy if you're willing to pay, but the lack of Apple TV spatial audio support is a glaring omission.
key_points: At 385 grams, AirPods Max are significantly heavier than competitors like Bose QC35 II (235g), making them impractical for exercise or vigorous movement
The all-metal construction (aluminum cups, stainless steel headband) makes every plastic competitor feel cheap by comparison, similar to Apple's unibody laptop transition
Spatial audio is genuinely immersive and transformative for movie watching, but its limitation to iPhone/iPad sources — excluding Apple TV — is a baffling omission for a $550 product
The lack of an on/off switch and the minimalist Smart Case (pouch, not hard case) are quintessentially Apple design choices with real trade-offs
As travel headphones, they represent the opposite trade-off from AirPods Pro: heavier and pricier than Bose, but dramatically better sounding
money_quotes: At $550, they are indisputably expensive, and at 385 grams, they are undeniably heavy.
It's like when Apple started making laptops out of unibody aluminum — the plastic ones we had long accepted as the norm suddenly seemed cheapjack in comparison.
"Is it a $550 click?" I asked. He clicked it a few more times. "It's not *not* a $550 click."
Watching a movie with spatial audio is an experience.
It's less like wearing headphones and more like wearing an augmented reality audio headset.

It's crazy that Apple sells a premium movie-watching set-top box and very premium movie-listening wireless headphones and they don't work together for a premium spatial audio movie-listening experience.
Buying a pair of AirPods Max to use solely as wired headphones would be like buying an airplane to drive around on the ground.
If you think any wireless headphones could be worth $550, these might be them.
key_insight: AirPods Max are a genuinely premium product whose excellent build and audio quality are undermined by weight, price, and the inexplicable absence of spatial audio support on Apple TV — the one device where they'd make the most sense.
